front: Poem: compares parting lovers to "stiff twin compasses" — "Thy firmness makes my circle just"
Poem: opens "As virtuous men pass mildly away" and criticizes "dull sublunary lovers' love"
Poem: declares Death is "slave to fate, chance, kings, and desperate men" and tells it "thou shalt die"
back: Death, be not proud / Holy Sonnet X (John Donne)

front: Poem: asks God to "break, blow, burn and make me new"
back: Batter my heart, three-person'd God / Holy Sonnet XIV (John Donne)

front: Poem: the speaker is "like an usurp'd town" whose Reason is a weak "viceroy"
back: Batter my heart, three-person'd God / Holy Sonnet XIV (John Donne)

front: Poem: opens "For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love"
Poem: "a well-wrought urn becomes / The greatest ashes" inspired Cleanth Brooks's title
Poem: argues for physical intimacy using a creature that "sucked me first, and now sucks thee"
Poem: calls the lover's body "my America! my new-found-land" and instructs "license my roving hands"
back: To His Mistress Going to Bed (John Donne)
work: To His Mistress Going to Bed

front: Poem: lists impossible tasks including "Get with child a mandrake root" and "Go and catch a falling star"
back: Song: Go and catch a falling star (John Donne)

front: Author: Meditation XVII contains "no man is an island" and "never send to know for whom the bell tolls"
Author: secretly married Anne More; delivered "Death's Duel" as his final sermon while fatally ill as Dean of St. Paul's
Author: subject of a Joseph Brodsky elegy describing him as having "sunk in sleep"
